ADC -project
With the same immunofluorescence findings, why do some patients respond to 
treatment whereas others demonstrate little or no clinical response? Why does 
treatment efficacy often appear reduced in subsequent relapses?

What is an ADC
•ADCs combine: 
oa tumour -targeting antibody 
oa cytotoxic drug payload 
•Designed to selectively deliver 
chemotherapy to cancer cells 
•Aim to improve efficacy while 
reducing systemic toxicity
•Bystander Effect (Conjugate -
Dependent):
oDeath of neighbouring non -target 
cells following exposure to targeted 
therapy .
o Extends effect beyond target cells, but 
may increase off -target toxicity.

What does the literature say about ADCs in TME
• Extreme Spatial Heterogeneity ("Patchiness"): Target antigens are 
rarely uniform; tumors frequently display distinct "hot" and "cold" zones 
mere micrometers apart.
• Center vs. Margin Discordance: Antigen expression levels often vary 
significantly between the tumour core and the invasive margin, meaning 
single -core biopsies can misclassify a patient's true ADC eligibility.
• Physical Stroma Barriers Impede Penetration: Dense extracellular 
matrix and CAFs create physical barriers and high interstitial pressure, 
preventing bulky ADC molecules from physically reaching target -
positive cancer cells.
• Reliance on the "Bystander Effect": Because target expression is so 
fragmented, clinical efficacy heavily relies on ADCs with membrane -
permeable payloads that can diffuse out of the target cell to kill 
neighboring target -negative tumor cells.
• Temporal and Metastatic Instability: Spatial distribution and overall 
target expression are dynamic; they frequently evolve between primary 
and metastatic sites, or in response to prior therapies, necessitating re -
biopsy upon disease progression.
Goni, O., Klümper, N., Muñiz Moreno, M. D. M., Eckstein, M., & Kuppe, C. (2026). Spatial Multiomics Reveal Insights Into ADC Efficacy. European Journal of Immunology , 
56(5), e70190. https://doi.org/10.1002/eji.70190

. D. M., Eckstein, M., & Kuppe, C. (2026). Spatial Multiomics Reveal Insights Into ADC Efficacy. European Journal of Immunology , 
56(5), e70190. https://doi.org/10.1002/eji.70190
Li, X., Janik, T., Möbs, M., Florian, S., Schmitt, W. D., et al. (2025). Spatial, Temporal, and Molecular Heterogeneity of ADC targets in High -Grade Serous Ovarian 
Carcinoma. medRxiv (Preprint). https://doi.org/10.1101/2025.12.05.25341695There is not 
much published 
quality data on 
these questions!

ADCs in High -Grade Serous Ovarian Cancer
•HGSC is frequently diagnosed at advanced 
stages 
•Treatment resistance and recurrence are 
common 
•ADCs are emerging as promising therapies 
for platinum -resistant disease 
•Important ADC targets include: 
oFOLR1 (FRα) 
oTROP2 
oB7-H4

The reality is not that impressive

The reality is not that impressive
"Investigator -assessed progression -
free survival was significantly longer 
in the MIRV group (median, 5.62 
months; 95% CI, 4.34 to 5.95 ) than in 
the chemotherapy group (median, 
3.98 months; 95% CI, 2.86 to 4.47 ) 
(P<0.001) (Figure 1A). The restricted 
mean progression -free survival at 12 
months was 6.13 months (95% CI, 
5.62 to 6.64 ) in the MIRV group and 
4.72 months (95% CI, 4.21 to 5.23 ) in 
the chemotherapy group."
DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa2309169
Where is the efficacy of targeted 
chemo?

Unanswered Questions That May Explain 
Current Challenges
•To what extent is there inter -patient and intra -tumoural 
variation ?
oWhich form of variation is greater in comparison, and how might 
this influence treatment response and disease progression?
•What is the spatial composition of the tissue 
microenvironment?
oDistinct organisational patterns are observed in t -CyCIF analyses. 
What accounts for these differences, and how might they 
influence biological function and therapeutic efficacy?
•At present, immunohistochemistry staining alone is being 
utilised as the primary marker.
oWhich alternative or additional biomarkers may provide greater 
diagnostic or predictive value?
•What role do immune cells and their composition play in 
treatment response?
Where is the efficacy of targeted 
chemo?

Why this matters?
•Target expression differs between 
patients and tumour regions 
•ADC efficacy may depend on: 
otarget density 
otumour localisation 
oimmune microenvironment 
interactions 
•Spatial profiling may improve: 
opatient stratification 
otreatment response prediction 
ounderstanding of resistance 
mechanismsTo look into 
this, we 
have the 
following 
data 
modalities

What RNA expression could reveal?
•Antibody expression appears highly tumour -specific . 
•How variable is expression between different genes, 
both relative to each other and after normalisation? 
•What is the extent of inter -patient heterogene ity in 
expression patterns? 
•To what degree do multiple targets show co-expression 
within the same spatial regions or cells?
There seem to be a 
lot of variation at 
least in some genes

Assessing Intra -Patient Variation with GeoMX 
and scRNA -seq
As expected, the degree of 
inter -patient heterogeneity 
exceeded that of intra -
patient heterogeneity 
(Pöllänen et al.). 
Nevertheless, 
substantial intra -
tumoural
heterogeneity was 
still observed. Which source of variation is 
truly greater in magnitude? 
Which forms of 
variation are 
biologically or 
clinically significant? Are highly expressing cells 
spatially clustered within 
specific niches, or are they 
distributed randomly 
throughout the tissue ?

Assessing 
Spatial 
Organisation 
Using t -CyCIF
•How are antibodies spatially distributed 
across the tissue microenvironment ? 
oExpression patterns can be broadly 
categorised into regions that are: 
▪Positive for all antibodies 
▪Antibody -specific expression 
domains 
▪Antibody -negative regions 
•Intra -tumoural variation is also evident 
within these spatial expression patterns. 
•To what extent do spatial protein 
expression patterns correlate with bulk 
RNA expression profiles ? 
•Are there discernible differences in 
expression patterns between IDS and 
PDS samples ? 
•Do the observed expression patterns 
indicate the formation of distinct 
antibody -defined niches ?

Are there niches or is the composition just 
random?
•Do identified niches exhibit similar 
spatial proximity to immune cells and 
comparable levels of immune 
infiltration? 
oDoes niche formation or density influence 
local cellular behaviour or 
microenvironmental dynamics? 
oAre there differences in immune organisation 
between distinct expression -defined 
regions? 
oHow are these spatial niches integrated into 
broader immune interaction networks? 
•What proportion of the tumour is 
affected by the bystander effect? 
In courtesy of Ziqi Kang, and SPACE -project

WES 
•Is there an underlying genomic basis 
driving the observed spatial and 
phenotypic variation? 
oWhat is the role of homologous 
recombination deficiency in shaping 
these patterns? 
oHow does whole -genome duplication 
contribute to tumour heterogeneity and 
structure? 
oWhat is the association with fraction of 
genome altered ? 
oTo what extent does loss of 
heterozygosity influence these effects? 
oAre specific driver genes or mutational 
events associated with the observed 
spatial or expression phenotypes?
•Probably not , but this needs to be 
checked
Similar analysis to SPACE can be 
used to check this.

Xenium
•Extension of the integrative framework to 
Xenium spatial transcriptomics . 
•High -resolution mapping of ADC target –
immune interactions within the TME. 
•Characterisation of spatial organisation within 
tumour niches at transcriptomic resolution. 
•Linking gene expression programmes to 
spatially defined immune and tumour cell 
architectures. 
•Xenium reveals our answers on spatial 
trascriptomic level
